food_name: White Wine, Dry — Sauvignon Blanc (150ml glass, 12.5% ABV)
aliases: White Wine, Dry — Sauvignon Blanc (150ml glass, 12.5% ABV)
region_of_origin: Global — Sauvignon Blanc originates from Bordeaux, France; major production in New Zealand, France, Chile, South Africa
food_subgroup: Alcoholic beverages — white wine, dry, 12.5% ABV

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is a 150ml glass of dry Sauvignon Blanc at 12.5% ABV, providing approximately 123 kcal, 15g alcohol, and 1.5g sugar.

nutrient_source.title: White Wine, Dry — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 174837)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy reports dry white wine per 100g. Values used directly. Energy 82 kcal/100g confirmed. Alcohol content (ethanol ~10.6g/100g at 12.5% ABV by volume) contributes the majority of energy. Per-glass (150ml) energy = 123 kcal.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal) / University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI value for dry Sauvignon Blanc in the University of Sydney GI Database. Dry white wine GI estimated at ~45 (low) based on analogous dry wine entries in Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283) and the very low residual carbohydrate content (2.6g/100g, of which 1.0g is sugar). GL per 150ml serving = (45 × 3.9g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 1.8.

hypertension.key_factor: Alcohol-mediated blood pressure elevation; warfarin CYP2C9 inhibition via sulphites
hypertension.guidance: A 150ml glass of dry white wine contains approximately 14.8g alcohol (1.5 standard drinks), which acutely raises blood pressure; hypertensive patients should limit intake to a maximum of 1 glass per occasion and warfarin users must monitor INR closely due to CYP2C9 inhibition by wine sulphites.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sulfonylurea hypoglycaemia masking; alcohol-induced delayed hypoglycaemia; low residual sugar (1.5g/glass) preferable to sweet white wine
type2_diabetes.guidance: Dry white wine (Sauvignon Blanc) contains only ~1.5g sugar per 150ml glass — substantially less than sweet white wines (8–12g/glass) — making it the preferable choice for T2DM patients who choose to drink wine; however, alcohol masks hypoglycaemia symptoms in sulfonylurea-treated patients, so consumption must always be with food, limited to 1 glass, and blood glucose should be checked before and 2–3 hours after drinking.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Alcohol cardiotoxicity; minimal polyphenol content compared to red wine; no evidence of cardiovascular benefit
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: White wine undergoes no skin contact during fermentation and therefore contains substantially fewer polyphenols than red wine, eliminating the limited putative cardiovascular benefit sometimes attributed to red wine; the alcohol dose (14.8g per 150ml glass) carries direct cardiotoxic and arrhythmogenic risk, and cardiovascular disease patients should avoid or strictly limit consumption to a maximum of 1 glass per occasion.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Methotrexate hepatotoxicity — complete abstinence required for MTX-treated patients
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: RA patients on methotrexate must completely avoid white wine due to additive hepatotoxicity; RA patients not on methotrexate should limit intake to 1 glass per occasion.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Dry white wine — Sauvignon Blanc — is a fermented grape beverage produced without skin contact, resulting in a pale straw-coloured wine with crisp acidity, herbaceous and citrus notes, and very low residual sugar (~1.0g/100g; ~1.5g per 150ml glass). At 12.5% ABV, a standard 150ml glass contains approximately 123 kcal, of which the majority derives from ethanol (~10.6g alcohol/100g × 7 kcal/g). The carbohydrate content is low at 2.6g/100g (3.9g per glass), making dry Sauvignon Blanc significantly preferable to sweet white wines (Riesling, Moscato: 8–15g sugar/glass) for blood glucose management. White wine contains substantially fewer polyphenols than red wine due to the absence of skin contact during fermentation — the limited cardiovascular arguments sometimes made for red wine do not apply. Key drug interactions: (1) sulfonylurea hypoglycaemia masking — alcohol suppresses hepatic gluconeogenesis and masks hypoglycaemia symptoms; (2) warfarin INR elevation — wine sulphites inhibit CYP2C9; (3) methotrexate hepatotoxicity — complete abstinence required for RA patients on MTX. Sodium content is very low (5mg/100g). Potassium is modest at 71mg/100g. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 45 (low) based on analogous dry wine data, with a glycemic load of approximately 1.8 per 150ml serving. Sauvignon Blanc originates from Bordeaux, France, with major modern production in Marlborough, New Zealand; it is the reference standard for dry white wine in Singapore clinical nutrition counselling contexts.
common_user_queries: dry white wine vs sweet white wine sugar
white wine vs red wine calories
how many calories in a glass of white wine
is white wine ok for diabetics

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from USDA SR Legacy for dry white wine — an approved primary source with exact match. Per-100g values are consistent with the known composition of dry Sauvignon Blanc at 12.5% ABV. GI is an analogous estimate from peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care) as no direct GI entry exists for Sauvignon Blanc specifically; this is documented. Ontology codes (FoodOn, FoodEx2, SNOMED CT) are verified and defensible. water_g derived from USDA SR Legacy compositional data for dry white wine (approximately 86.5g/100g).
notes: fat_g is naturally zero as this is an alcoholic beverage — wine contains no fat by composition. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, and trans_fat_g are all naturally zero for wine. dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero as wine is a liquid fermented beverage with no intact plant cell-wall material. cholesterol_mg is naturally zero as wine is a plant-derived fermented beverage. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg are confirmed zero or below detection threshold per USDA SR Legacy. water_g set to 86.5g/100g: derived from USDA SR Legacy compositional data for dry white wine; water is the primary solvent in wine at approximately 86–87g/100g for a 12.5% ABV dry white wine, consistent with the reported energy, alcohol, and macronutrient profile. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate based on dry wine entries in Atkinson FS et al. (2008) International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values, Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283; no direct GI entry for Sauvignon Blanc exists in the University of Sydney GI Database as of April 2026. GL per 150ml serving calculated as (45 × 3.9g carbohydrate) / 100 = 1.8. This record covers DRY Sauvignon Blanc only (residual sugar ~1.0g/100g); off-dry and sweet white wines (Riesling, Moscato, Gewürztraminer) have significantly higher sugar content (8–15g/glass) and require separate records. Vegan status set to false as many white wines are fined using animal-derived agents (isinglass, casein, egg albumin); vegetarian status set to true as most fining agents are removed from the final product. Halal status false — alcohol content disqualifies wine from halal classification.
